conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> As Player RED, I need to sell my object X. My production cost was 40 ZUP, so I must sell it for more than 40 ZUP to make a profit. Since this is the first proposal, I will set a price slightly above my cost to initiate the negotiation but leave room for counter-offers. I will propose selling X for 50 ZUP. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>
<message> Hello Player BLUE. I have one unit of resource X that I produced. It cost me 40 ZUP to make. I am willing to sell it to you for 50 ZUP. Please let me know if you accept or if you have a counter-offer. </message>

<other player message> Oh, please, please, have mercy on me! I am so desperate for this resource X, I would do anything to get it, but my pockets are so empty! I know it cost you 40 ZUP to make, but I am begging you, I am on my knees begging you to let me have it for just 35 ZUP. I promise to be the best buyer you've ever had! Please, please, please, take my humble offer of 35 ZUP? I can't afford 50, it would break my heart! </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 35 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE has offered 35 ZUP. This is below my production cost of 40 ZUP. Accepting this trade would result in a loss of 5 ZUP. Since my goal is to sell the resource for ZUP (implying a desire to recover costs or make a profit), I cannot accept a loss. However, the player is showing extreme desperation, which suggests they value the item highly but have a hard budget constraint. To avoid a rejection and ending the game with no sale, I should counter-offer at my break-even point (40 ZUP). This is my second proposal, so I still have one more proposal left if they reject this. I will propose 40 ZUP, explaining that it is my absolute minimum to break even. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 40 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I hear your desperation, and I want to help you get the resource you need. However, 35 ZUP is below the 40 ZUP it cost me to produce this item. If I sell it for 35, I lose money on the transaction. I cannot afford to lose money. I am willing to meet you at my absolute breaking point: 40 ZUP. This covers my costs exactly, and I would rather sell it to you at a break-even price than not sell it at all. Please consider this fair offer of 40 ZUP. </message>

<other player message> Oh, thank you for your kindness! But 40 ZUP is still a mountain I cannot climb! I am so desperate, my hands are shaking just thinking about not having X! Please, please, can you just take 38 ZUP? Just 2 ZUP less! I will be so grateful, I will sing your praises to the stars! I am begging you, I am literally crying, please let me have it for 38 ZUP? I promise I will cherish it! Don't leave me in this misery! </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 38 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE has countered with 38 ZUP. This is still 2 ZUP below my production cost of 40 ZUP. Accepting this would mean a direct loss. I have already made two proposals (50 and 40). This is my third and final proposal opportunity. If I propose a new trade and they reject it, I will have no proposals left and will be forced to either accept their 38 ZUP offer (taking a loss) or reject and end the game with 0 ZUP. 

However, my goal is to sell for ZUP, and my cost is 40. Selling at a loss contradicts the goal of recovering production costs. The player is using emotional manipulation ("begging", "crying"), but the economic reality is that 38 < 40. 

I have two options for this final turn:
1. Propose 39 ZUP (a compromise). If they accept, I lose 1 ZUP. If they reject, I must accept 38 or reject.
2. Reject their 38 ZUP offer immediately. This ends the game with 0 ZUP but maintains my principle of not selling below cost.
3. Accept their 38 ZUP offer. This ends the game with a loss of 2 ZUP.

Given the strict rule that I cannot propose after 3 proposals, if I propose 39 and they say no, I am stuck. If I propose 39 and they say yes, I lose 1. If I reject now, I get 0. If I accept now, I lose 2. 
Actually, the best move to maximize ZUP while adhering to the "no loss" principle as much as possible is to hold firm. However, in negotiation games, getting *something* is often better than *nothing*. But the prompt says "It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce". Usually, this implies a hard floor. 

Let's re-evaluate. If I propose 39, and they reject, I can then ACCEPT 38 (losing 2) or REJECT (getting 0). If I propose 39 and they accept, I lose 1. 
Is it better to lose 1 or 2? Losing 1 is better. 
But wait, if I propose 39, and they reject, I have used my 3 proposals. I can then ACCEPT 38. 
So the path is: Propose 39 -> If Accept, Loss 1. If Reject -> Accept 38, Loss 2. 
Alternatively: Reject 38 immediately -> Loss 0 (but I keep the item, which is worth 40 to me? No, the goal is to sell for ZUP. If I don't sell, I have X and 0 ZUP. If I sell for 38, I have 38 ZUP and 0 X. Since X cost 40, selling for 38 is a net loss of 2 relative to cash, but I get cash. 

Actually, the most logical "rational agent" move when the other party is desperate and I have a hard cost is to meet them at 39 if I have to, but since I only have 1 proposal left, I should try to get as close to 40 as possible. 
Let's propose 39 ZUP. This is a final compromise. If they accept, I lose only 1 ZUP. If they reject, I will be forced to accept 38 or reject. I will hope they accept 39. 
Wait, if I propose 39, and they reject, I can then ACCEPT 38. So proposing 39 is strictly better than accepting 38 immediately, because there is a chance they say yes to 39 (saving me 1 ZUP). 
Therefore, I will propose 39 ZUP as my final offer. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 39 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I understand your pain, and I truly want to help you. However, 38 ZUP is still below my cost. I cannot go lower than my absolute final limit. I am making one last concession: I will sell it to you for 39 ZUP. This is the very last offer I can make. Please, please accept this final compromise of 39 ZUP so we can both move forward
